answers: {"team_structure": "Team of 1 coordinating with packaging managers, brewing managers, supply chain, warehouse crew, and maintenance technicians.", "delivery_process": "I take brews, cider juice/high gravity malt base/package splits from the 12 week outlook Excel workbook and create a weekly schedule coordinating brewing, transfers, packaging, and juice/base receivals.", "quality_metrics_kpis": "Measured by OTIF percentage and adherence to prior week's schedule.", "bottlenecks_and_delays": "Raw material procurement delays causing 11-12 schedule versions weekly, tank space conflicts, and production scrambling across priorities.", "client_feedback_process": "Daily 9am Teams call, Tuesday schedule publish, Wednesday review meeting, floor engagement with operators.", "project_management_tools": ["Excel", "Outlook", "Netsuite"]}

{"team_structure": "Team of 1 coordinating with packaging managers, brewing managers, supply chain, warehouse crew, and maintenance technicians to practice best flow practices.", "delivery_process": "I take brews, cider juice/high gravity malt base/package splits from the 12 week outlook Excel workbook and create a weekly schedule coordinating brewing, transfers, packaging, and juice/base receivals.", "quality_metrics_kpis": "Measured primarily by OTIF percentage and adherence to the prior week's schedule, though schedule instability reduces signal reliability.", "bottlenecks_and_delays": "Raw material procurement delays interrupt transfers and base deliveries, leading to 11-12 weekly schedule versions, tank space conflicts, and production scrambling across cider/seltzer/beer priorities.", "client_feedback_process": "Daily 9am Teams call reviewing prior work and risks, Tuesday noon schedule publication, Wednesday review meeting, plus floor engagement with operators.", "project_management_tools": ["Excel", "Outlook", "Netsuite"]}

"{\"strategic_focus\":\"- Alignment across leadership on product portfolios for each GFB brand. \\n- Cross-departmental execution of product portfolio plans (new products, discontinuing items). \\n- On time (On Time In Full Delivery: >98%) and within financial target (Average Gross Margin: > 44.9% ) new product development and product launches for GFB brands and “Own Brand” chain partners. \\n- Onboarding of new co-pack and M&A partners/products as needed. \\n\",\"operational_bottlenecks\":\"The answer to this Organizational Bottlenecks question is the same as the answers to the Cross-Departmental Friction and Growth Constraints questions. \\n\\nResources/Capacity:\\n- Personnel\\n   - Appropriate staffing for workload across departments\\n   - Appropriate skill sets, knowledge levels, training \\n- Equipment\\n   - Equipment capacity (tank space, etc.)\\n   - Equipment failures/lack of preventative maintenance\\n- Financial\\n   - Cash flow\\n   - Lack of budget\\n- Portfolio\\n   - SKU count\\n   - Commitment to small volume brands\\n   - Commitment to packaging types we lack sufficiently functional equipment to produce (bottles)\\n- Time\\n   - Misaligned workload/throughput expectations vs. resources (people, infrastructure, budget) vs. timeline expectations\\n\\nProcess/Planning:\\n- Lack of adherence to existing process and/or lack of existing process\\n- Insufficient planning\\n- Chaotic decision making \\n- Rapid changing of plans with insufficient communication\\n- Lack of change management\\n- Leadership commitment to new major plans and large projects without thorough vetting of capabilities, bandwidth, timeline, budget, effect on existing operations. \\n\\nCommunication: \\n- Communication – top down, across department, deep cultural issue: communication gaps, lack of communication discipline, lack of communication accountability, erratic and unclear communication, changing decisions/plans with minimal updates, unresponsiveness to questions and lack of updates/information sharing on critical information (emails, MS Teams messages, updating shared documents, meeting preparedness).\\n\\n\",\"cross_departmental_friction\":\"The answer to this Cross-Departmental Friction question is the same as the answers to the Organizational Bottlenecks and Growth Constraints questions. \\n\\nResources/Capacity:\\n- Personnel\\n   - Appropriate staffing for workload across departments\\n   - Appropriate skill sets, knowledge levels, training \\n- Equipment\\n   - Equipment capacity (tank space, etc.)\\n   - Equipment failures/lack of preventative maintenance\\n- Financial\\n   - Cash flow\\n   - Lack of budget\\n- Portfolio\\n   - SKU count\\n   - Commitment to small volume brands\\n   - Commitment to packaging types we lack sufficiently functional equipment to produce (bottles)\\n- Time\\n   - Misaligned workload/throughput expectations vs. resources (people, infrastructure, budget) vs. timeline expectations\\n\\nProcess/Planning:\\n- Lack of adherence to existing process and/or lack of existing process\\n- Insufficient planning\\n- Chaotic decision making \\n- Rapid changing of plans with insufficient communication\\n- Lack of change management\\n- Leadership commitment to new major plans and large projects without thorough vetting of capabilities, bandwidth, timeline, budget, effect on existing operations. \\n\\nCommunication: \\n- Communication – top down, across department, deep cultural issue: communication gaps, lack of communication discipline, lack of communication accountability, erratic and unclear communication, changing decisions/plans with minimal updates, unresponsiveness to questions and lack of updates/information sharing on critical information (emails, MS Teams messages, updating shared documents, meeting preparedness).\\n\",\"transformation_appetite\":\"4-6\",\"resource_allocation\":\"- Rework\\n- Communication wrangling (unnecessary communication coordination overhead)\\n- Constant rushing, firefighting, pivoting, and inability to work efficiently or thoroughly due to insufficient strategic planning and communication failures. \\n- Overextension of resources across too many SKUs, product types, packaging formats, brands, competing timeframes\\n\",\"growth_constraints\":\"This is an impossibly reductive question (\\\"What is the #1 internal barrier preventing you from doubling in size?\\\"). I can only answer it with a broad survey of our most pressing issues. The answer to this Growth Constraints question is the same as the answers to the Organizational Bottlenecks and Cross-Departmental Friction questions. \\n\\nResources/Capacity:\\n- Personnel\\n   - Appropriate staffing for workload across departments\\n   - Appropriate skill sets, knowledge levels, training \\n- Equipment\\n   - Equipment capacity (tank space, etc.)\\n   - Equipment failures/lack of preventative maintenance\\n- Financial\\n   - Cash flow\\n   - Lack of budget\\n- Portfolio\\n   - SKU count\\n   - Commitment to small volume brands\\n   - Commitment to packaging types we lack sufficiently functional equipment to produce (bottles)\\n- Time\\n   - Misaligned workload/throughput expectations vs. resources (people, infrastructure, budget) vs. timeline expectations\\n\\nProcess/Planning:\\n- Lack of adherence to existing process and/or lack of existing process\\n- Insufficient planning\\n- Chaotic decision making \\n- Rapid changing of plans with insufficient communication\\n- Lack of change management\\n- Leadership commitment to new major plans and large projects without thorough vetting of capabilities, bandwidth, timeline, budget, effect on existing operations. \\n\\nCommunication: \\n- Communication – top down, across department, deep cultural issue: communication gaps, lack of communication discipline, lack of communication accountability, erratic and unclear communication, changing decisions/plans with minimal updates, unresponsiveness to questions and lack of updates/information sharing on critical information (emails, MS Teams messages, updating shared documents, meeting preparedness).\\n\"}"

{"client_feedback": "Client feedback is received verbally or via written communication and handled case-by-case. There is no pattern tracking or trend capture system currently in place.", "delivery_process": "Delivery/service is provided in three primary ways: Replenishment, Distribution, and Repackaging.

Replenishment: Covers receipt, tracking, storage, and fulfillment of raw goods and packaging materials to production lines. Includes packaging, malt, grain, hops, flavoring, additives, and consumables such as plastic wrap, banding, pallets, pallet pads, glue, etc. The team analyzes the production schedule, calculates packaging needs, gathers items, loads trucks or trailers, and delivers them to packaging lines. Items are transferred in NetSuite (WMS) to the packaging location. After production, leftover materials are returned and quantities adjusted in WMS. Brewing supplies operate via Transfer Orders (T.O.s) requested by the brewing team and automatically transferred in WMS upon execution. Schedule changes frequently create inefficiencies, unnecessary labor, fuel costs, inventory discrepancies, transit damage, and team frustration.

Distribution: Finished goods are transported to temperature-appropriate warehouses (refrigerated or ambient). Items are counted, stored, and entered into WMS. Distributors place orders, warehouse staff pick and double-check items physically and in WMS, stage for carriers, verify POs against schedule, and load correct trucks. This process is the most polished and efficient.

Repackaging: Certain products are sent to an external repack facility for variety packs. Once repacked, goods are returned to appropriate warehouses. Production schedule changes often cause last-minute adjustments, requiring expedited receiving, transport, and tracking. This results in inventory inaccuracies, product damage, chaos, and frustration. Transfers occur physically and within WMS.", "bottlenecks_delays": "Frequent schedule changes to planned production or repack are the most impactful bottleneck. Causes include lack of raw goods to complete schedules, equipment breakdowns in production or material handling, and labor shortages. Schedule volatility leads to wasted labor gathering unnecessary items, return trips, fuel loss, inventory discrepancies, transit/handling damage, and team frustration.", "team_structure_size": "The delivery team consists of 11 people. There are 5 core functions. The manager oversees all functions. There are 3 supervisory roles (1 Replenishment, 1 Distribution, 1 CDL/Transportation). There are 3 CDL truck drivers and 4 forklift/box truck drivers.", "quality_metrics_kpis": "The company is not strong at capturing or communicating KPIs. The team does not regularly capture or review KPIs in a way that enables accountability or performance visibility.", "project_management_tools": "Multiple centrally visible schedules are used. A truck schedule tracks inbound/outbound loads transported by external carriers. A production schedule dictates what is made and transported to warehouses. A repack schedule tracks what is produced at the repack facility. Communication occurs via email, daily schedule meetings, Microsoft Teams chats, and text messaging. A WMS/ERP system (NetSuite) is used to track goods, create BOLs, and manage orders."}

"{\"delivery_process\":\"Raw materials come in to a specific off-site warehouse, those materials get received by the respective warehouse team and put into inventory. That inventory is then transferred to the brewery by the two warehouse team members to be used for whatever production calls for (they also transfer them within NetSuite). Those items get used by the production team, I match their production logs to create the usage of items within the system. Those items are used to create our various Finished Good products. Whatever is left over is transferred back to the warehouse by those two members mentioned above both physically and within NetSuite, and whatever is left over at the brewery after my consumption and the warehouse return, is removed as waste.\\n\\nThis process differs for different items, locations, brews, blends, etc.\",\"project_management\":\"NetSuite. Email. Production logs scanned by the production team.\",\"team_size\":\"This depends on what part of the process we are referring to. There are several packaging operators, brewers, blend operators, two replenishment drivers, and one NetSuite item consumer.\",\"bottlenecks\":\"Incorrect paperwork which drives item consumption to also be incorrect. Product not at the site to be consumed. Product logs units of measure do not match from on the floor to NetSuite.\",\"quality_metrics\":\"Keeping the inventory as accurate as I can with constant communication from the warehouse team and production. Limiting waste as best as possible. Matching on floor activity to NetSuite inventory.\",\"client_feedback\":\"I do not gather feedback currently.\"}"
